title: Macquarie could disrupt big four's 'lazy' $370b edge
full_text:
The nation's four major banks have almost $370 billion in low or zero-interest customer accounts that have underpinned their funding and profit growth, but the model is under growing pressure from rival Macquarie as it grabs market share with its higher interest savings products.
If the major banks move to become more competitive with Macquarie and other lenders on savings products, analysts have warned the implications for their earnings could be significant, with a potential hit to earnings of up to 40 per cent, in a worst-case scenario modelled by analysts at UBS.
"It's obviously a massive moat the big four banks have around their businesses at the moment ... and a key component of their competitive advantage," said UBS analyst John Storey.
Jarden analyst Matthew Wilson said the banks had $370 billion of "lazy deposits" that millions of Australians have parked with ANZ, Commonwealth Bank, NAB, and Westpac. "The point is, why do we lend our money to banks for nothing?"
Wilson said Westpac chief executive Anthony Miller agreed with him. "He's told his retail bank to think about a scenario where there are no low-cost deposits."
Miller and the chief executives at the other major banks are under pressure to shore up their market share, as customers increasingly seek better returns on deposits.
The big four offer low base interest rates on most of their savings accounts and higher bonus rates if a customer meets certain requirements, such as growing the account balance each month or making a certain number of transactions.
A survey earlier this month by consumer research group Canstar found that about 40 per cent of customers fail to meet the bonus conditions required to earn the highest rate of interest. The practice has drawn political scrutiny.
Macquarie's standard transaction account pays a 2.75 per cent interest rate. At its full-year results in March, Macquarie reported its deposit book had grown to $215 billion - a 25 per cent increase over the 12 months, compared with industry-wide growth of about 8 per cent.
CBA chief executive Matt Comyn was grilled on low-interest rates on savings accounts at a parliamentary hearing in November. At the time, he said: "We've put a lot of effort into alerting customers, such that we can maximise the proportion of customers who are getting that bonus interest."
Comyn, who leads Australia's largest bank, has been reluctant to engage in a price war on deposits, preferring to rely on CBA's brand and vast retail branch network to defend its market-leading position.
But analysts argue the rapid rise in Macquarie's deposit book is a threat to the big banks.
Deposits are the most crucial source of funding for banks, which they use to help write loans, and any fall in their value has an impact on the bank's profit margins.
UBS modelled the earnings impact of two scenarios in which 70 per cent of each major bank's non-interest-earning customers were switched to either a 5 per cent deposit interest rate or a more modest 3 per cent return.
Under their modelling, the biggest loser would be Westpac, where 14 per cent of the bank's customer deposits are non-interest-bearing - the highest proportion of the big four.
If Westpac were to pay a 5 per cent rate on those deposits, its earnings per share could be cut by 37 per cent, UBS found. ANZ's earnings would fall 35 per cent, CBA's 27 per cent and NAB's 23 per cent.
UBS analyst John Storey said the 5 per cent model is an extreme scenario, but even under the more modest 3 per cent, there is still a potential earnings hit of between 14 and 22 per cent.
Minh Pham, an analyst at fund manager Milford, which invests in the major banks, said the issue is of concern to bank chief executives. "The profitability of the deposit book would look quite different if they did that [follow Macquarie's strategy], that's the conundrum for them," she said.
"It definitely is a concern from an investor just looking at the whole banking sector. Macquarie has been able to disrupt all lines of profitability."
Macquarie's strategy is being replicated by other banks across the sector, according to Canstar.
AMP offers a 5.1 per cent ongoing savings rate for balances up to $500,000. Smaller lender Easy Street, the digital arm of Community First Credit Union, offers a 5.05 per cent rate on balances up to $3 million. CBA subsidiary Bankwest offers 5 per cent for balances up to $250,000. None of these offers is contingent on any monthly conditions.
Jarden's Wilson has modelled the impact on the big four if deposits paying zero or very little interest were repriced at the prevailing cash rate (4.35 per cent). Commonwealth Bank would face a 0.75 per cent hit to its net interest margin, followed by Westpac at 0.52 per cent, National Australia Bank at 0.44 per cent and ANZ at 0.2 per cent.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Angira Bharadwaj
heading: Banking and Economy
